title: Chapter 38: The Confrontation
content: The espresso hits me before I even open the door — that dense, almost burnt sweetness that every café in this city claims is artisanal and authentic. This place is no different. Exposed brick. Reclaimed wood. The performative rusticity of the Garment District's latest wave of coffee shops, built on the bones of actual working factories. I know because I used to source from the building next door.

I push through the door and the smell thickens, layered now with steamed milk and something yeast-dough sweet from the pastry case. The bell above the door chimes. A few heads turn. They always turn — I've designed for that reaction my entire life. But today is different. Today the heads turn for a reason I could never have sketched on paper.
I'm wearing THE CONVERSATION.

My doublet-bomber fusion. The piece that started as a provocation and became a thesis statement — structured shoulders dissolving into a cropped athletic hem, jacquard meeting technical nylon, the historical and the hypermodern stitched into one garment that refuses to resolve. I tailored it to this body three days ago. My own hands. My own measurements. The fabric remembers my needle even when my skin doesn't remember its own shape.

The suit moves differently on me now. The weight of the bomber falls across shoulders that are narrower than last month's. The waist suppression I built into the doublet panels catches a silhouette that didn't exist four weeks ago. Every step I take is a proof of concept. The designer wearing their own argument: that clothing is a conversation between body and world, and that both parties get a vote.
I see her immediately.

Maren Voss, corner table, laptop open, half a macchiato going cold beside her. She's dressed like she's trying to look like she's not trying — black turtleneck, no jewelry, the kind of deliberate anonymity that is itself a signature. Her fingers pause over the keyboard. She looks up.
Her face does something I will replay for the rest of my life — however long that turns out to be.

Recognition first. She knows my face — or she knows a version of it. The bone structure is the same, the set of the jaw, the way I hold my chin like I'm daring someone to try to lower it. But the proportions have shifted. The architecture of me has been renovated without moving the load-bearing walls. She sees me, and then she doesn't see the me she expected, and then she sees me again through whatever internal database she's been building for months.

Confusion second. A visible hitch. Her eyebrows pull together for maybe half a second before she kills the expression. But I see it. I was trained to see the micro-reactions that happen before the mask settles. It's what makes me good at fitting — reading the body before the mind can dress it in politeness.

Calculation third. Her eyes track down my body — the suit, the way it fits, the way it shouldn't fit this well on someone who supposedly isn't what the world thinks they are. I watch her compute. I watch her recategorize. I watch her decide that whatever she thought she knew about Orlando Garros has just been outdated.
"You look different," she says.
She says it flat. A statement, not a question. Professional. The way a journalist observes an evidence shift without committing to what it means.
"I am different," I say.

I pull out the chair across from her and sit down. I don't ask. I don't wait for the gesture. The chair scrapes against the concrete floor and the sound is loud enough that someone two tables over glances our direction, then looks away. Maren closes her laptop halfway — the protective gesture of someone who doesn't want their screen read. I don't care about her screen. I care about her attention. I have it now.
"I'm not here to threaten you," I say. "I'm not here to beg. I'm here to offer you something."

She leans back. She's good at neutrality — her face is a locked door. But there's something behind it. A wire pulled taut. She's been waiting for this. Maybe not this exact version of me, but this confrontation. She's been building toward it the way I build toward a collection: research, material, vision, execution.
"What could you possibly offer me?"

I feel the fabric of the doublet shift as I rest my forearms on the table. The jacquard is warm from my body heat. It shouldn't be this comfortable — this body hasn't worn it before. But cloth has memory too. Or maybe I have memory enough for both of us.
"The truth," I say. "The whole truth. Exclusive. Before anyone else."

Her eyes sharpen. There it is — the thing that drives her, the engine under the hood. Not malice. Not ambition in the crude sense. Hunger. For the story. For the thing that will outlast her byline, that will be cited and referenced and taught in journalism classes when the hot takes have crumbled to digital dust. Maren Voss is a predator, yes, but she's a predator who kills for meaning, not for sport.
"Why would you give me that?"

The macchiato sits between us like a still life. She hasn't touched it since I arrived. I can smell the espresso residue on the rim of her cup — sharp, slightly over-extracted, the way they make it in places that prioritize aesthetic over craft. My mouth carries the bitter ghost of the coffee I drank before walking in here, a double ristretto I downed more for ritual than taste, and the sourness of it lingers under my tongue like a premonition.
"Because you're going to write about me anyway," I say.

She doesn't deny it. She can't. We both know what she's been building. ORLANDO — IMPOSSIBLE. I've seen the file references. I've seen the way her sources tighten when my name comes up. I've felt the shape of the story she's been assembling — a monster story, built from Dmitri Valk's blueprint. Impossible designer. Anomaly. Something to fear. Something that shouldn't exist, and therefore must be exposed, contained, explained away.

"And I'd rather the story be mine than yours," I continue. "Your version — the Dimitri Valk version — is a monster story. 'Impossible Designer. Anomaly. Something to fear.' But the real story is bigger and more human than that. And you know it."

I pause. I let the silence work. The café fills it — the hiss of the steam wand, the low murmur of a couple arguing about subway routes, the percussive tap of someone else's keyboard. All of it pressing against the quiet between us.
"You've been researching me for months," I say. "You've seen the evidence. You've felt it — that this isn't just a scandal. This is a story that could change how people think about identity itself."
Maren's fingers rest on the closed lid of her laptop. Her nails are trimmed short, no polish. Efficient. Unadorned. She doesn't confirm anything. She doesn't deny anything. She waits.

She is so good at waiting. I've known four centuries of patience, and Maren Voss could teach lessons. But I've come prepared to wait longer. I've come prepared to be the most interesting thing in this room, in this district, in this city — until she has no choice but to engage.
"Here's my offer," I say. "You write the story — my story, in my words, with my cooperation. Exclusive access. Photographs. Documentation. Everything."

I watch her face for the flicker. It comes — a micro-expression, a softening around the eyes that she catches and kills in under a second. But it was there. The predator scenting something better than prey.

"But you write it honestly," I say. "Not as a monster story. Not as a freak show. As a love story that spans four hundred years and asks what it means to be a person when your body won't stay the same."

The words hang between us. I've said the number. Four hundred years. Out loud, to a journalist, on the record. I've done the thing you're never supposed to do — hand the blade to the person pointing it at you and trust that the angle of the light will change what they see when they look at it.

Maren's silence has a texture. I've been cataloguing silences since before the Enlightenment, and hers is a rare one. It's not the silence of denial or the silence of decision. It's the silence of recalibration. She's running the numbers. She's mapping the angles. She's taking the weapon she spent months forging and holding it up to the light to see if it might be more useful as a mirror.
"And what about the people who say you're lying?"
She asks it without cruelty. She asks it the way a surgeon asks about allergies — information she needs before she cuts.
"I have four hundred years of evidence," I say. "And I just shifted in front of the man I love."

I feel the fabric of the doublet press against my sternum as I breathe. The weight of it. The rightness of it. This body in this garment, making this argument, at this table, across from this woman. There is nothing I have ever designed that fits as perfectly as this moment.
"Let them call me a liar," I say. "I'll call them small."
Something moves in her face. Not a softening — she doesn't soften. A realignment. The way a compass needle wobbles before it finds a truer north than the one it was pointing at.
She picks up her macchiato. She takes a slow sip. The cup returns to the saucer with the quiet click of a decision being deferred, not denied.
"Why should I trust you?" she asks.
The question is a test. Not of honesty — nobody trusts a stranger's honesty. Of intelligence. Of self-awareness. Of whether I understand what I'm asking and what it costs.
"You shouldn't," I say. "That's what makes this interesting."

For the first time since I sat down, Maren Voss smiles. It's small. It's involuntary. She catches it between her teeth and kills it, but not before I see it — the spark of a journalist who has just been handed a story bigger than the one she came for, and who recognizes that the real risk isn't being deceived. The real risk is being boring.

I stand. The suit moves with me — fabric and body in conversation, exactly as I designed. I don't leave a card. I don't give her a deadline. Maren Voss doesn't work on other people's timelines, and I've just demonstrated that I know that. The offer sits on the table between us like an unclaimed hand in a card game. She'll pick it up. Or she won't. But she'll think about it. She'll think about it tonight when she goes back to her ORLANDO — IMPOSSIBLE file and reads through the weaponized fragments she's been assembling, and she'll feel the shape of them change in her hands. A monster story that could be a love story. A takedown that could be a revelation. A career footnote that could be the defining work of her life.

I push through the café door and the bell chimes again, and the June air hits me — warm, humid, carrying the faint chemical sweetness of the dye houses a few blocks south. The street is loud with the Garment District's midday chaos: delivery trucks double-parked, hand trucks rattling over sidewalk grates, a cluster of design interns smoking and scrolling outside a sample room.

Rowan is in the car, parked at the corner with the window down. He didn't come in. I asked him not to. This was my fight — not because I don't trust him beside me, but because the narrative has to be mine. If Maren Voss writes this story, she has to write about me, not about us. The love is the heart of it, but the voice has to be mine. The ownership has to be mine. He understood before I finished explaining.

I open the passenger door and slide in. The leather seat is warm from the sun through the windshield. Rowan puts his phone down. His eyes move over my face — reading me, the way he always does, the way he's done across centuries and bodies and names. He doesn't ask if I'm okay. He knows I'm okay. He asks something better.
"How did it go?"

I pull the door shut. The street noise dims to a murmur. In the sudden near-silence of the car, I hear the residual espresso grinding in my thoughts, the phantom click of Maren's cup returning to its saucer, my own pulse settling into a steadier rhythm now that the high-wire act is behind me.
"I think I just hired my biographer."

Rowan's mouth does something that isn't quite a smile — more like the memory of a smile, or the anticipation of one. He starts the car. We pull away from the curb, and I watch the café disappear in the side mirror, and I think about Maren Voss sitting at that corner table with an offer she can't stop thinking about and a file she may never publish in its original form.

I chose narrative ownership over defensive warfare. I could have fought the exposure — threatened, pleaded, litigated, fled. I've done all of those things across four hundred years, and every single time, the story won. The story always wins. It flows like water through every wall you build, every legal injunction, every carefully crafted denial. The only way to survive a flood is not to build higher walls. It's to change the shape of the channel.

The monster story becomes a love story. The predator becomes the witness. And Maren Voss — the most dangerous journalist in New York, the woman with the file and the sources and the byline that could end me — is standing at a crossroads I built for her, holding a weapon I just taught her to hold differently.
Whether she takes the role of witness instead of executioner will determine everything.

Not just for me. For everyone who will come after. Every person whose body won't stay the same, who has lived inside a story written by someone else, who has been catalogued as impossible by people too small to imagine otherwise. If Maren writes my story — the real story, the human story — she won't just be writing about Orlando Garros. She'll be writing a door that others can walk through.

Rowan's hand finds mine on the console between the seats. His grip is warm and certain. Four hundred years, and his hand still fits mine the way fabric fits a body — not because the measurements never change, but because the conversation between us never stops.

I watch the Garment District scroll past the window. Fabric stores and button suppliers and sample rooms full of dress forms waiting to be somebody. Every one of them a body that hasn't been decided yet. Every one of them a conversation that hasn't started.

I started one today. In a café, across from a woman who wanted to end me, I started the most important conversation of my life — not by defending what I am, but by offering to explain it. Not by fighting the story, but by telling it better.
Let them call me impossible.
I'll show them inevitable.
